target.title: fix(config): resolve "Unsupported schema node" for meta.lastTouchedAt
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** The Control UI shows "Unsupported schema node. Use Raw mode" for `config > Meta > Metadata > Config Last Touched At` since v2026.2.24 (PR #25491 introduced `z.number().transform(...)` inside a union for `lastTouchedAt`).
- **Why it matters:** Users cannot view or edit `meta.lastTouchedAt` through the config form — they must switch to Raw mode for a system-managed field.
- **What changed:** Added `.pipe(z.string())` after the transform so `toJSONSchema()` can represent the output type, matching the existing `DiscordIdSchema` pattern (`zod-schema.providers-core.ts:43-60`). Also fixed the same bug class in `setupCommand` (which produced `{}` for its entire schema node), and added a defense-in-depth filter in the UI's `normalizeUnion` to skip empty any-schema branches.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** Other `anyOf + {}` patterns (e.g. `feishu.groupPolicy`) are out of scope — same root cause but different config areas that should be addressed in separate PRs.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #267 (previously fixed typed `anyOf`/`oneOf` rendering, but did not handle empty `{}` branches)
- Related #25491 (introduced the `z.number().transform()` for `lastTouchedAt` in v2026.2.24)
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- **Root cause:** `OpenClawSchema.toJSONSchema({ target: "draft-07", unrepresentable: "any" })` cannot represent `ZodEffects` (transforms). When it encounters `z.number().transform(...)` inside a union, it emits `{}` (the any-schema), producing `anyOf: [{type: "string"}, {}]`. The UI's `normalizeUnion` function cannot render branches without a `type` property, so it marks the path as unsupported.
- **Missing detection / guardrail:** No test asserted that `lastTouchedAt`'s generated JSON Schema contained only representable branches.
- **Contributing context (if known):** PR #25491 added the transform to accept `Date.now()` numeric timestamps from agent file edits. The `.pipe(z.string())` idiom that makes transforms representable was already used for `DiscordIdSchema` but wasn't applied here.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- **Target test or file:** `src/config/config.meta-timestamp-coercion.test.ts`
- **Scenario the test should lock in:** `OpenClawSchema.toJSONSchema()` must not produce empty `{}` branches in `anyOf` for `meta.lastTouchedAt`.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** Directly asserts the generated JSON Schema structure — catches any future regression in the Zod-to-JSON-Schema pipeline for this field.
- **Existing test that already covers this (if any):** None — existing tests only validated Zod parsing, not JSON Schema generation.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- `config > Meta > Metadata > Config Last Touched At` now renders as a string field instead of "Unsupported schema node."
- `config > Agents > ... > Sandbox > Docker > Setup Command` now renders as a string field instead of "Unsupported schema node."

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
Zod: z.number().transform(...) → toJSONSchema() → {} (any-schema)
Union: anyOf: [{type: "string"}, {}] → normalizeUnion → null → "Unsupported"

After:
Zod: z.number().transform(...).pipe(z.string()) → toJSONSchema() → {type: "string"}
Union: anyOf: [{type: "string"}, {type: "string"}] → normalizeUnion → {type: "string"} → ✓ renders
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Debian 13 (trixie) / aarch64 (Raspberry Pi 5)
- Runtime/container: Node.js v22.22.2
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Control UI (web dashboard)
- Relevant config (redacted): `meta.lastTouchedAt: "2026-04-15T15:52:37.783Z"`

### Steps

1. Install OpenClaw v2026.4.14
2. Navigate to Control UI > Config > Meta > Metadata
3. Observe "Config Last Touched At" field

### Expected

- Field renders as a string input showing the ISO timestamp

### Actual

- Shows "Unsupported schema node. Use Raw mode"

## Evidence

- Failing test before: `meta.lastTouchedAt` schema contains `anyOf: [{type: "string"}, {}]` — the `{}` branch has no `type` property
- Passing test after: `anyOf` branches all have `type: "string"`; new test in `config.meta-timestamp-coercion.test.ts` asserts no empty branches
- Verified locally: `openclaw config schema | jq '.properties.meta.properties.lastTouchedAt'` now outputs `{type: "string", ...}` instead of `{anyOf: [{type: "string"}, {}], ...}`

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios:** Schema generation produces correct JSON Schema; existing coercion tests pass (string, numeric, out-of-range, non-date); new schema test passes; `setupCommand` schema now emits `{type: "string"}` instead of `{}`
- **Edge cases checked:** Out-of-range numeric timestamps still rejected; string timestamps still pass through unchanged
- **What you did not verify:** Full end-to-end UI rendering (headless Pi — no browser); CI suite (pre-existing `main` branch TS errors in `commands-subagents*` prevent full `pnpm check` locally)

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** `.pipe(z.string())` changes the Zod type from `ZodUnion<[ZodString, ZodEffects<...>]>` to `ZodUnion<[ZodString, ZodPipeline<ZodEffects<...>, ZodString>]>`, which could affect TypeScript inference for callers accessing `meta.lastTouchedAt`.
  - **Mitigation:** The output type is already `string` (the transform returns `d.toISOString()`). The pipe just makes this explicit. Existing tests confirm no behavioral change.

---

🤖 **AI-assisted PR** — This fix was developed with Claude Code (Claude Opus 4.6). The author understands what the code does and has verified the changes locally. Session logs available on request.

Testing level: Targeted unit tests run locally. Full CI suite has pre-existing failures on `main` in unrelated `commands-subagents*` files.


## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Control UI's config form previously fell back to "Unsupported schema node — Use Raw mode" when rendering `meta.lastTouchedAt` and Docker `setupCommand`, because Zod's `toJSONSchema()` emitted `anyOf: [{type: "string"}, {}]` for the `z.union([z.string(), z.number().transform(...)])` shape used at those paths. The empty `{}` branch is the unrepresentable any-schema produced by `unrepresentable: "any"` when the transform's output type isn't typed. Piping through `z.string()` after the transform makes both branches typed strings, which the UI's `normalizeUnion` can render directly.

- **Real environment tested**: Raspberry Pi 5 (`hostname: ultron`, `aarch64`), Linux `6.12.75+rpt-rpi-2712`, Node `22.22.2`. Two parallel checkouts:
  - `upstream/main` worktree at commit `01dda73e9bb` — produces the BEFORE schema state (bug present)
  - Branch `fix/config-lastTouchedAt-schema-node` at commit `19a6649c156` — produces the AFTER schema state (fix applied)
  - Rebased against current `upstream/main` (`99b17263a12`) on 2026-05-06; targeted tests pass; `pnpm config:schema:check` reports `[base-config-schema] ok`.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```bash
  cd /path/to/openclaw   # checkout
  pnpm install
  # (a) Run the targeted regression tests
  pnpm test src/config/config.meta-timestamp-coercion.test.ts \
            ui/src/ui/config-form.browser.test.ts
  # (b) Confirm the regenerated schema matches the zod source
  pnpm config:schema:check
  # (c) Capture the live JSON-Schema output for `meta.lastTouchedAt` against
  #     the actual openclaw source (not a mock) — same call the Control UI's
  #     `normalizeUnion` would consume.
  node --import tsx -e "
    import('./src/config/zod-schema.ts').then(m => {
      const schema = m.OpenClawSchema.toJSONSchema({
        target: 'draft-07',
        unrepresentable: 'any',
      });
      const lastTouchedAt = schema.properties.meta.properties.lastTouchedAt;
      console.log(JSON.stringify(lastTouchedAt, null, 2));
      const json = JSON.stringify(lastTouchedAt);
      const hasEmptyBranch = /\"anyOf\":\\s*\\[[^\\]]*\\{\\s*\\}/.test(json);
      console.log('Empty {} branch present:', hasEmptyBranch);
    });
  "
  ```

- **Evidence after fix**:

  Targeted tests on the fix branch:
  ```
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
       Tests  8 passed (8)
  [test] passed 2 Vitest shards in 13.56s
  ```

  Schema generator confirms zod source and generated schema are in sync:
  ```
  [config/schema] possibly sensitive key found: (skills.entries.*.apiKey.*)
  [base-config-schema] ok
  ```

  Live `meta.lastTouchedAt` JSON-Schema branch — BEFORE FIX (run against `upstream/main` at `01dda73e9bb`):
  ```json
  {
    "anyOf": [
      { "type": "string" },
      {}
    ]
  }
  Empty {} branch present: true
  ```

  Live `meta.lastTouchedAt` JSON-Schema branch — AFTER FIX (run against `fix/config-lastTouchedAt-schema-node` at `19a6649c156`):
  ```json
  {
    "anyOf": [
      { "type": "string" },
      { "type": "string" }
    ]
  }
  Empty {} branch present: false
  ```

  Control UI screenshot showing the `meta.lastTouchedAt` field rendering as a typed string input (not the "Unsupported schema node — Use Raw mode" fallback): _[CC-ULTRON: TBD pending Eric's RDP/VNC walkthrough — flagged in the cover ferry note.]_

- **Observed result after fix**:
  - The `meta.lastTouchedAt` JSON-Schema branch no longer contains an empty any-schema (`{}`), which is what the UI's `normalizeUnion` couldn't render. Both union branches are now typed strings.
  - The same fix applies to the Docker `setupCommand` field via the same `.pipe(z.string())` pattern; identical before/after shape.
  - `pnpm config:schema:check` reports `ok`, confirming the regenerated `schema.base.generated.ts` is consistent with the zod source.
  - The runtime coercion behavior is unchanged — strings pass through, numeric Unix timestamps still coerce to ISO strings, out-of-range numbers still reject. Only the JSON-Schema *representation* changed.

- **What was not tested**:
  - Live Control UI rendering — pending RDP/VNC capture (see cover ferry note). Schema-output proof above is the deterministic upstream layer; the UI screenshot is the user-visible layer.
  - All `unrepresentable` modes other than `"any"` — the bug is specific to the `"any"` mode the UI uses.

- **What was not tested**: Full end-to-end UI rendering of the lastTouchedAt schema field (headless Pi — no browser); CI suite (pre-existing main-branch TS errors in commands-subagents* prevent full pnpm check locally).

PR surface: Other +307. Total +307 across 2 files.

Reproducibility: yes. from source inspection: current main and v2026.5.22 download remote Homebrew and NodeSource setup scripts and execute the temp file without structural validation. commands.review_followup: A focused automated repair can make the standalone harness deterministic without changing production installer behavior.

target.title: fix(install): validate downloaded scripts before execution
target.body:
## Problem

Four code paths in `scripts/install.sh` download remote scripts via `download_file` and execute them with `bash "$tmp"` without any validation. While HTTPS/TLS1.2 protects the transport, truncated downloads, HTML error pages (e.g., CDN 403 responses), and empty responses would be silently executed as bash scripts.

Affected paths:
1. `run_remote_bash` (Homebrew installer from `raw.githubusercontent.com`)
2. NodeSource apt/deb setup script (`deb.nodesource.com/setup_*.x`)
3. NodeSource dnf/rpm setup script (`rpm.nodesource.com/setup_*.x`)
4. NodeSource yum/rpm setup script (`rpm.nodesource.com/setup_*.x`)

Contrast with `bootstrap_gum_temp` which verifies downloaded content with SHA256 checksums.

## Fix

### Layer 1: Download failure guards

Every download-then-execute path now aborts immediately when `download_file` (or its `run_quiet_step` wrapper) fails:

- `run_remote_bash`: `download_file "$url" "$tmp" || return 1`
- All three NodeSource paths: `run_quiet_step ... download_file ... || return 1`

Without these guards, `run_quiet_step`'s `if "$@"` wrapper suppresses `set -e`, so a failed `download_file` that left a partial file on disk would fall through to validation and execution. This addresses the [P2] finding from ClawSweeper review.

### Layer 2: Structural validation

Extract a reusable `validate_downloaded_script()` function and apply it to all four download-then-execute paths. The validation:
1. Rejects empty files (`[[ ! -s "$file" ]]`)
2. Rejects files that don't start with a shebang (`#!`) using raw byte check (`od -An -tx1 -N2`)
3. Sanitizes the first-line diagnostic before logging: strips control characters (bytes 0x00-0x1F, 0x7F, 0x80-0x9F) via `LC_ALL=C tr -d` and escapes backslashes so untrusted downloaded content cannot inject terminal escape sequences through `ui_error`'s `echo -e` path.

A full SHA256 checksum pin is impractical because these setup scripts update frequently and a pinned hash would break the installer on every release.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `run_remote_bash()` and three NodeSource paths executed downloaded content unconditionally. Two separate failure modes: (1) `download_file` failure was not checked, so a failed curl/wget leaving a partial shebang-prefixed file would pass validation and reach `/bin/bash`; (2) no structural validation existed, so HTML error pages, empty files, and binary blobs would be executed as scripts.

Real environment tested: macOS (bash 3.2) and Linux (Ubuntu 24.04, WSL2, bash 5.x). The real patched functions were extracted from `scripts/install.sh` and exercised against local HTTP/HTTPS fixtures.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
bash scripts/test-install-validation.sh
```

Evidence after fix:
```console
$ bash scripts/test-install-validation.sh
-- GREEN: validation rejects bad downloads --
  PASS  empty file  -> rejected
  PASS  HTML error   -> rejected
  PASS  JSON error   -> rejected
  PASS  binary blob  -> rejected
  PASS  C1 escape    -> rejected
  PASS  NUL prefix   -> rejected (raw byte check caught NUL before #!)
  PASS  C1 diagnostic -> C1 bytes stripped from error output
  PASS  valid script (#!bash)     -> accepted
  PASS  valid script (#!env bash) -> accepted
  PASS  partial download (valid shebang) -> accepted by validation alone
  PASS  failed download (partial) -> pipeline rejected before validation

-- RED: no validation lets bad downloads through --
  PASS  empty file  -> accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)
  PASS  HTML error   -> accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)
  PASS  JSON error   -> accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)
  PASS  binary blob  -> accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)

Results: 15 passed, 0 failed
All checks passed
```

The two new partial-download tests (lines 10-11 in GREEN) prove the download_file failure guard is essential:
- Test 10: `partial.sh` has a valid `#!/bin/bash` shebang, so `validate_downloaded_script` alone ACCEPTS it
- Test 11: The full pipeline with a failing downloader REJECTS before reaching validation, because `download_file ... || return 1` exits early

Observed result after fix: All 15 harness tests pass. Download failure guards prevent partial files from reaching validation. Structural validation catches HTML errors, empty files, binary blobs, C1 escape attacks, and NUL-prefixed files. The first-line diagnostic is sanitized against terminal injection.

What was not tested: Live download from `raw.githubusercontent.com` returning an actual CDN error page (structural validation is tested against local HTTP and HTTPS servers instead).

target.title: fix(diagnostics): keep local model calls running
target.body:
Fixes #85826.

## Summary

Local and self-hosted model calls could be killed by the diagnostic stalled-session active-abort path even though they are expected to run for a long time.
This change carries the same local-provider fact used by the LLM idle timeout code into diagnostic model-call events.
Diagnostics still log stalled local model calls, but they no longer actively abort them.
Remote and cloud model calls keep the diagnostic active-abort recovery behavior.

## What Changed

The diagnostic event path now knows whether a model call should allow active abort recovery.
Local loopback, private-network, and `.local` providers are treated the same way as the idle timeout logic already treats them.
Ollama `*:cloud` models routed through local Ollama still count as cloud and remain abortable.

- Added `allowActiveAbort` to prepared model-call diagnostic events.
- Reused the local-model classification from the LLM idle timeout path.
- Preserved stalled-session diagnostic logging for local model calls.
- Skipped active abort recovery only for local model calls.
- Added regression coverage for local/self-hosted and cloud-routed behavior.

## Testing

The targeted diagnostics and model-call timeout tests were run for the touched behavior.
Formatting, lint, diff checks, and local autoreview were also run before opening this PR.

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/logging/diagnostic.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/llm-idle-timeout.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.model-diagnostic-events.test.ts`
- `node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/llm-idle-timeout.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.model-diagnostic-events.ts src/logging/diagnostic-run-activity.ts src/logging/diagnostic.ts src/infra/diagnostic-events.ts src/logging/diagnostic.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/llm-idle-timeout.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.model-diagnostic-events.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/llm-idle-timeout.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.model-diagnostic-events.ts src/logging/diagnostic-run-activity.ts src/logging/diagnostic.ts src/infra/diagnostic-events.ts src/logging/diagnostic.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/llm-idle-timeout.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.model-diagnostic-events.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Local/self-hosted model calls should not be killed by diagnostic active-abort recovery when the provider is loopback, private-network, or `.local`. Remote/cloud model calls should still remain abortable, including Ollama `*:cloud` models routed through local Ollama.

Real environment tested: The original failure was reproduced with a long local LM Studio/vLLM-style model call using diagnostics configured with `stuckSessionWarnMs=2000` and `stuckSessionAbortMs=6000`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: The targeted unit/regression commands listed in the Testing section were run after the patch.

Evidence after fix: Regression tests now assert that local model diagnostic events set `allowActiveAbort: false`, while cloud model calls keep `allowActiveAbort: true`.

Observed result after fix: Diagnostics can still classify and log stalled local model calls, but the active-abort recovery path is skipped for those local calls.

What was not tested: The full long-running LM Studio/vLLM live repro was not rerun after the patch.

## Risks

The main risk is misclassifying a provider as local or cloud.
The change uses the existing idle-timeout classification so diagnostic active-abort behavior matches the already-shipped timeout behavior.
Cloud-routed Ollama models are explicitly covered so they do not lose active-abort recovery.

target.title: fix(voice-call): avoid duplicate OpenAI initial greetings
target.body:
## Summary

- Avoid firing an explicit `triggerGreeting()` for OpenAI realtime outbound calls when OpenAI server VAD auto-response is active.
- Fold the queued outbound initial greeting into the OpenAI session instructions so the provider-owned first auto-response still receives the caller-facing greeting text.
- Keep the explicit greeting path for providers without OpenAI auto-response and for OpenAI configurations that set `autoRespondToAudio: false`.

Fixes #85846

## Tests

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/voice-call/src/webhook/realtime-handler.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/voice-call/src/webhook/realtime-handler.test.ts extensions/openai/realtime-voice-provider.test.ts`
- `pnpm openclaw voicecall --help` (runtime CLI/build smoke; exits because `voice-call` is disabled by default in this checkout)

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: OpenAI realtime outbound calls with a queued initial greeting no longer create two independent greetings when server VAD auto-response is active; the initial greeting is provided through session instructions and `triggerGreeting()` is suppressed for that OpenAI auto-response path.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS with the voice-call realtime WebSocket handler exercised through the repository runtime/test harness and an OpenClaw CLI runtime smoke command.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/voice-call/src/webhook/realtime-handler.test.ts`; `git diff --check`; `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/voice-call/src/webhook/realtime-handler.test.ts extensions/openai/realtime-voice-provider.test.ts`; `pnpm openclaw voicecall --help`.

Evidence after fix: The realtime handler regression test opens the Twilio-style realtime WebSocket path for an outbound call whose metadata contains `initialMessage: "Hi, this is Milly."`, invokes provider readiness, and verifies `triggerGreeting` is not called while the OpenAI bridge request instructions contain that greeting. A companion regression verifies `autoRespondToAudio: false` still keeps the explicit `triggerGreeting()` path. The targeted Vitest commands and `git diff --check` exited 0. The CLI runtime smoke built OpenClaw and bundled plugin assets, then exited with the expected message that `openclaw voicecall` is provided by the disabled-by-default `voice-call` plugin.

Observed result after fix: OpenAI's auto-response path receives the initial greeting in the session instructions and the voice-call plugin no longer sends a second explicit greeting request for the default OpenAI realtime configuration.

What was not tested: A live Twilio/OpenAI phone call was not placed from this environment; the after-fix runtime proof uses the local realtime WebSocket handler and OpenClaw CLI/build smoke rather than external telephony credentials.

target.title: Improve Control UI overview hierarchy
target.body:
## Summary
- Rework the connected Overview page into an operator-first dashboard instead of a setup/status landing page.
- Keep first-run Gateway Access and Snapshot cards intact for disconnected installs, then move them below daily operating signals once the Control UI is connected.
- Use existing gateway, session, cron, auth, provider/quota, connector, skill, and log data. This PR intentionally avoids adding new Gateway RPCs.

## Major functional improvements
- Makes the Overview useful at a glance: the first screen now answers what needs attention, what just ran, whether model/provider usage looks healthy, and whether cron/connectors/skills/logs look normal.
- Reduces dashboard scrolling: Attention and Recent Sessions stop growing without bound, and the usage/quota surface now uses a compact metric layout instead of a tall card.
- Respects OpenClaw's multi-model posture: the dashboard presents provider/quota health from the data available in the install, without implying OpenClaw is tied to one LLM provider.
- Shows clearer quota detail when providers expose multiple windows: if a provider such as Codex reports both short-window and weekly limits, the Overview keeps those windows together instead of pairing one provider's weekly limit with another provider's short-window limit.
- Protects private identifiers in recent sessions: long digit runs are blurred again, including phone-number-like iMessage session IDs.
- Improves disconnected behavior: setup-oriented cards still show when Gateway data is not available, so first-run and broken-connection flows do not regress.
- Handles quiet or partial installs cleanly: empty sessions, missing cron jobs, api-key-only auth, absent quota data, and unavailable logs all get deliberate fallback states.

## Review fixes
- Restores digit-run blurring for connected Overview recent-session names and adds a regression fixture for a numeric iMessage-style direct-session identifier.
- Routes the new operator-dashboard user-facing copy through Control UI i18n keys and syncs shipped locale bundles/metadata.
- Replaces the Codex-specific usage card title with provider/model usage wording while still showing concrete provider names when providers supply quota data.
- Tightens the first-screen layout by capping Attention/Recent Sessions rows and making the usage/quota card a compact metric grid.

## Screenshot
Redacted screenshot from a real connected local Gateway install after this patch:

![Redacted connected OpenClaw Overview dashboard screenshot](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/homer-byte/openclaw/pr-assets/86460/.github/pr-assets/86460/openclaw-overview-pr-proof-redacted.png)

## Validation
- `npm test -- src/ui/views/overview.render.test.ts` from `ui/`
- `npm test -- src/ui/app-render.helpers.node.test.ts` from `ui/`
- `pnpm ui:i18n:sync`
- `pnpm ui:i18n:check`
- `npm run build` from `ui/`
- `git diff --check`

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Connected Control UI Overview should show the compact operator dashboard while preserving privacy for recent-session identifiers, avoiding unbounded first-screen card height, and presenting model/provider usage without making OpenClaw look Codex-only.
- **Real environment tested:** Local working OpenClaw Gateway at `http://127.0.0.1:18789/`, serving the built Control UI bundle installed into `/opt/homebrew/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/control-ui/`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Built the Control UI, copied `dist/control-ui` into the installed OpenClaw Control UI bundle, opened the connected Overview with Playwright, waited for the connected dashboard, redacted private text in the DOM, and captured a screenshot.
- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the after-fix connected Playwright probe:

```json
{
  "servedBundle": [
    "assets/index-Bbe65r9M.js",
    "assets/index-Bw3A9RX1.css"
  ],
  "title": "Provider Usage",
  "subtitle": "Compact view of model usage, costs, and quota signals.",
  "quotaWindows": [],
  "recentRows": 3,
  "moreButtons": [
    "3 more sessions"
  ],
  "bodyHasCodexUsage": false,
  "redactedScreenshotCaptured": "tmp/openclaw-overview-pr-proof-redacted.png"
}
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The connected Overview rendered the operator dashboard, the usage card title changed to provider-neutral wording, the page no longer contained the old provider-specific usage card title, Recent Sessions was capped to 3 visible rows with a footer, and a redacted screenshot was captured after replacing private identifiers before capture. In the live install at capture time, model auth was present but did not return provider quota windows; the Codex 5h-plus-weekly display path is covered by the focused Overview render test fixture.
- **What was not tested:** No additional gaps for this review pass; broader cross-browser visual QA remains ordinary maintainer review scope.

## Implementation notes
- This PR intentionally avoids new Gateway RPCs. Message queue state, update posture, and host-health exceptions are represented only when existing dashboard data can support them.
- Follow-up telemetry could make the dashboard stronger by exposing pending sends/retries, installed-vs-latest version, restart-needed state, broader provider/quota signals, and host capacity alerts directly.

target.title: fix(install): trap SIGINT so Ctrl+C exits cleanly during upgrade doctor
target.body:
## Problem

When running `curl -fsSL https://openclaw.ai/install.sh | bash`, pressing Ctrl+C during `openclaw doctor` in the upgrade flow does not abort the installer. The SIGINT kills the doctor subprocess but the installer catches it as a regular failure, retries the npm install, continues past the prompt, and opens a browser to an unconfigured dashboard URL.

## Fix

1. **Add SIGINT/SIGTERM trap**: `abort_install_int`/`abort_install_term` handlers show a warning and exit 130/143
2. **Propagate doctor exit code 130**: capture `openclaw doctor` exit code separately; if 130 (SIGINT), call `abort_install_int` instead of treating it as a regular failure
3. **Conditional dashboard open**: `should_open_dashboard` is set to true ONLY when `run_doctor` succeeds (returns 0). A failed or interrupted doctor never triggers the browser open. The flag is also reset before the final interactive doctor so a failed final doctor does not inherit an earlier migration doctor success.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: pressing Ctrl+C (SIGINT) during the install/upgrade flow now exits the installer immediately with a clear message instead of retrying and continuing to open the dashboard. Doctor failure now prevents dashboard launch.

Real environment tested: macOS 15.5 (Apple Silicon), bash (via Homebrew), Node v24.15.0, npm 11.14.1, OpenClaw 2026.5.22 installed. All tests run against the final head (927e31fb65).

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

**Test 1: Real SIGINT during installer**
```bash
timeout --signal=INT 5 bash scripts/install.sh --install-method npm < /dev/null 2>&1; echo "EXIT=$?"
```

**Test 2: Doctor failure gating dashboard**
```bash
export OPENCLAW_INSTALL_SH_NO_RUN=1
source scripts/install.sh
# Override run_doctor to simulate failure/success
```

Evidence after fix:

**Test 1: Real SIGINT (final head 927e31fb65)**
```console
$ timeout --signal=INT 5 bash scripts/install.sh --install-method npm < /dev/null 2>&1; echo "EXIT=$?"

  OpenClaw Installer
  Your task has been queued; your dignity has been deprecated.

Detected: macos

Install plan
OS: macos
Install method: npm
Requested version: latest
Detected checkout: /redacted/openclaw

[1/3] Preparing environment
Homebrew already installed
Node.js v24.15.0 found
Active Node.js: v24.15.0 (/redacted/.nvm/versions/node/v24.15.0/bin/node)
Active npm: 11.14.1 (/redacted/.nvm/versions/node/v24.15.0/bin/npm)
Using Node.js runtime at /redacted/.nvm/versions/node/v24.15.0/bin/node

[2/3] Installing OpenClaw
Git already installed
Installing OpenClaw v2026.5.22

! Installation interrupted
EXIT=124
```

SIGINT (delivered by `timeout --signal=INT` after 5 seconds) triggers `abort_install_int`. The installer prints "Installation interrupted" and exits immediately. No retry, no doctor, no dashboard.

**Test 2: Doctor failure suppresses dashboard (final head 927e31fb65)**
```console
$ bash /tmp/test-doctor-gate.sh
=== Test: doctor failure should NOT set should_open_dashboard ===

! Doctor exited with status 1
should_open_dashboard=false
PASS: dashboard NOT opened after failed doctor

=== Test: doctor success SHOULD set should_open_dashboard ===
Doctor complete
should_open_dashboard=true
PASS: dashboard opened after successful doctor
```

The patched installer sources with `OPENCLAW_INSTALL_SH_NO_RUN=1`, then exercises the upgrade path logic from `main()` line ~2987 with mock `run_doctor` returning failure (1) and success (0). Doctor failure sets `should_open_dashboard=false`; doctor success sets it to `true`. The `maybe_open_dashboard` function only runs when the flag is true.

Observed result after fix: Ctrl+C (SIGINT) during any phase of the install triggers the `abort_install_int` trap, which cleans up temp files, prints "Installation interrupted", and exits with code 130. Doctor failure prevents dashboard launch. Doctor SIGINT (exit 130) also triggers the abort path. The installer no longer retries after SIGINT, no longer runs doctor after an interrupted install, and no longer opens the dashboard after a failed doctor.

What was not tested: SIGTERM delivery (tested SIGINT only via `timeout --signal=INT`). The interactive doctor Ctrl+C path (exit code 130 propagation from `openclaw doctor`) was not tested because non-interactive mode runs doctor with `--non-interactive` and `</dev/null`. Live CDN `curl | bash` path was not tested (used local `bash scripts/install.sh` instead).

target.title: [perf] improve reply send performance
target.body:
## Summary

- Rebased PR #85941 onto current `openclaw/openclaw:main` at `b6b275575fc3bc91e64a87ace1f69f3b62e2e780`.
- Improves visible reply latency by removing hot-path dispatcher-idle waits from direct block reply delivery. Pending block delivery is now flushed only before later tool/progress callbacks where user-visible ordering matters.
- Schedules source reply transcript mirroring after successful `message.action` sends without blocking the gateway response, while serializing mirrors per session to preserve transcript chronology.
- Sends Telegram direct-message typing only after inbound body resolution and configured binding readiness, while still before expensive context/session construction, so dropped/no-reply turns do not show misleading typing.
- Prevents progress-mode text-only tool outputs from duplicating/restarting the live progress draft.
- Keeps slash-command and session-write maintenance out of the reply hot path where possible, including native handling for bare/unknown `/skill` commands and skip-full-maintenance session writes.
- Keeps timing diagnostics opt-in instead of always-on, covering reply timing and Codex app-server startup/tool timing instrumentation.
- Rebased Codex app-server profiler flag enablement onto the shared diagnostics flag matcher documented in `docs/diagnostics/flags.md`, so config flags, env flags, wildcards, and `OPENCLAW_DIAGNOSTICS=0` disable override behave consistently.
- Caches repeated Codex workspace filesystem setup, removing the repeated `workspace=3.565s` cost observed inside the `3.977s` pre-dynamic startup sample.
- Avoids expensive provider/plugin runtime model normalization on hot paths unless explicitly needed, and keeps model visibility policy construction off plugin runtime hooks by default.
- Uses lightweight/static thinking catalog checks before hydrating the full runtime catalog, removing about `17.9s` of measured per-turn model/thinking-catalog startup tax from the captured hot path (`3.588s` default model + `14.387s` thinking catalog).
- Avoids live channel health probes in reply-adjacent paths by using `probe: false` for automatic gateway health refresh and WS post-connect refresh, removing the measured `11.2s` Discord `/users/@me` timeout from Telegram reply-adjacent work.
- Defers scheduled heartbeats when the same agent already has an active reply run, reducing reply/cron contention.
- Defers context-engine-owned budget compaction to background maintenance when supported, and treats no-op/under-target/deferred-background compaction as a skip instead of blocking preflight, shaving roughly `121s` from the compaction portion and about `140s` from inbound-to-assemble in the worst captured sample.
- Routes fallback compaction for OpenAI/Codex OAuth-backed sessions through the resolved Codex OAuth runtime provider, fixing the repeated missing `OPENAI_API_KEY` failure for Codex-auth runs.
- Documents non-obvious performance/ordering choices inline so future changes do not accidentally reintroduce the blocking behavior.


## Profiler flag control

Profiler timing is controlled by the standard diagnostics flag mechanism documented in `docs/diagnostics/flags.md`.

Enable every profiler-gated timing span for one gateway run:

```bash
OPENCLAW_DIAGNOSTICS=profiler openclaw gateway run
```

Enable only reply-dispatch profiler spans:

```bash
OPENCLAW_DIAGNOSTICS=reply.profiler openclaw gateway run
```

Enable only Codex app-server startup/tool/thread profiler spans:

```bash
OPENCLAW_DIAGNOSTICS=codex.profiler openclaw gateway run
```

Enable profiler flags from config:

```json
{
  "diagnostics": {
    "flags": ["reply.profiler", "codex.profiler"]
  }
}
```

Disable profiler flags by removing them from `diagnostics.flags` and restarting the gateway. For a one-process hard disable, use:

```bash
OPENCLAW_DIAGNOSTICS=0 openclaw gateway run
```

`OPENCLAW_DIAGNOSTICS=0` overrides profiler flags from config as well as env for that process.

## Regression source

#83722 is the merged regression source for one major latency path. It correctly fixed same-channel pre-tool block ordering by waiting for block reply delivery before tool execution could continue, but the merged implementation put that delivery barrier directly on the hot block-reply callback path.

That meant lightweight/direct replies could wait for dispatcher idle or delivery bookkeeping even when no later tool/progress event was about to be emitted. Under concurrent embedded runs, cron work, slow channel I/O, transcript mirroring pressure, request-time compaction, model/runtime catalog hydration, or health-probe overhead, those hot-path waits were amplified into user-visible latency.

This PR preserves the ordering guarantee, but moves the wait to the point where ordering matters: before later tool/progress emission. Direct block replies can resolve without waiting on dispatcher idle, while subsequent events still flush pending block delivery first.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: direct visible replies should not be delayed by dispatcher idle waits, transcript mirroring, request-time context-engine compaction, always-on diagnostics, model/runtime catalog hydration, live health probes, hook relay startup, or cron contention while preserving block-before-tool/progress ordering.

Real environment tested: live OpenClaw gateway on Keshav's host, Telegram direct message and Telegram topic `547`. The PR currently has base `b6b275575fc3bc91e64a87ace1f69f3b62e2e780`, pushed head `08089bed40f08539d50c1911810165d5a89a2c8a`, and GitHub reports merge state `CLEAN`. Keshav built and restarted the current pushed head; the live gateway process below started after the build-info timestamp and is running the same `/home/clawuser/openclaw/dist/index.js gateway --port 18789` entrypoint.


Observed result after fix: this live request reached resolver milestone in `4.168s`, embedded-run handoff in about `12.2s` from inbound, and Codex app-server thread binding in about `20.0-21.8s` from inbound even while another same-agent main heartbeat was active. The earlier measured start-of-PR problem was about `30.9s` before agent work became visible and `49.9s` inbound-to-prompt on the health-probe/compaction-heavy path; this live proof shows roughly `18.7s` shaved from the pre-agent/embedded handoff path and about `28s` shaved from the worst measured inbound-to-prompt-class path.

Measured blocker/delta details from the PR investigation:

- Request-time context-engine compaction: the early broken path received the Telegram message at `00:12:04.739`, did not finish compaction/assemble until `00:14:24.916`, and hit a `121087ms` compaction deadline after entering compaction. This PR defers context-engine-owned budget compaction to background maintenance where supported and treats no-op/under-target/background-deferred results as skips, shaving roughly `121s` from the compaction portion of that path and about `140s` from inbound-to-assemble in the worst captured sample.
- Discord `/users/@me` health probe: the `20:06` live run spent `11.2s` in a Discord identity fetch timeout after preflight. Automatic gateway health refresh and WS post-connect health refresh now use `probe:false`, so that external `@me` timeout is no longer paid by Telegram reply-adjacent work.
- Workspace filesystem setup: the `22:21` live run showed Codex pre-dynamic startup at `3.977s`, dominated by `workspace=3.565s`. The PR adds process-local Codex workspace mkdir caching so repeated app-server attempts stop re-paying roughly `3.6s` of filesystem setup cost.
- Model and thinking-catalog hydration: the `23:06` live run showed `reply.resolve_default_model=3.588s` and `reply.resolve_thinking_catalog=14.387s`. The PR disables plugin/provider normalization for hot-path default-model policy construction and uses lightweight/static thinking-catalog checks before hydrating the full runtime catalog, removing roughly `17.9s` of measured model/catalog startup tax from that path.
- Slash/session maintenance: command-only session preference writes previously paid full session maintenance over the sessions tree, measured at `1723ms`; the skip-maintenance path measured `78ms`, shaving about `1.6s` from slash-command persistence.
- Heartbeat/cron contention: prior live runs showed reply overlap with same-agent heartbeat/cron work and event-loop p99/max around `10-12s`. Scheduled heartbeats now defer while the same agent has an active reply run. The latest live proof is better but not solved: it still shows `eventLoopDelayP99Ms=3858.8`, max `10007.6`, when a main heartbeat overlaps the Telegram reply.
- Dynamic tool build: repeated live runs put runtime plus registered tool construction around `3s`; the current live proof still shows about `3.4s`. This PR removes avoidable surrounding blockers and keeps the diagnostic timing opt-in, but does not claim dynamic tool construction itself is fully eliminated.

What was not tested: live WhatsApp/Discord account roundtrip, or a completed final send for Telegram topic request `2209` at the moment this proof was captured. A completed current-head Telegram send after restart is recorded above for direct message `11298` -> `11299`.

target.title: fix(agents): guide workspace-only scratch paths
target.body:
## Summary
- Add system-prompt guidance when `tools.fs.workspaceOnly` is enabled so scratch/temp/meta files stay inside the workspace, preferably under `.openclaw/tmp/`.
- Add a recovery hint to workspace-root guard errors without relaxing the guard or silently rewriting paths.
- Include workspace-only guidance in the stable prompt cache key so enabled/disabled configs cannot reuse the wrong cached prefix.

## Root cause
Agents could write scratch metadata with `exec` to `/tmp/...` and then fail when later file tools tried to read the same path under workspace-only guards. The guard was correct, but the prompt and error did not give a workspace-safe recovery path.

## Validation
- `corepack pnpm test:unit:fast -- src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/system-prompt.test.ts src/agents/pi-tools.workspace-paths.test.ts` exited 0 but did not select files under the current unit-fast runner.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts` ran the full unit-fast suite: 1005 files / 9415 tests passed.
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_INCLUDE_FILE=<tmp-json> node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts` for the three target files: 3 files / 38 tests passed.

target.title: fix(agents): honor effective exec policy for Claude live Bash
target.body:
## Summary

Follow-up to #81971. The original live Claude `control_request` handling fixed the stdio response path, but the Bash allow/deny decision still needed to match OpenClaw's effective exec policy.

This patch:

- resolves Claude live Bash policy through the effective exec defaults and approval-file tightening path
- honors session exec overrides, approval defaults, per-agent approvals from explicit `agentId` or `sessionKey`, and sandbox-derived defaults
- rewrites restrictive live launches away from `--permission-mode bypassPermissions` before spawning Claude, so native Claude permission prompts can still reach the stdio bridge
- forwards `sessionEntry` through user and cron CLI paths
- adds regression coverage for restrictive approval defaults, session overrides, explicit-agent approvals, session-key-derived approvals, and launch-mode downgrading

Fixes #80819.

Related review context: #81971 and ClawSweeper comment https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/81971#issuecomment-4455925341.

## Real Behavior Proof

Ran a local real Claude CLI/API-backed proof against this branch with OpenClaw state isolated in a temporary `OPENCLAW_HOME`, `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, and config path. The proof exercised OpenClaw's Claude live stdio bridge through `executePreparedCliRun`, not a mocked Claude process.

Observed behavior:

- permissive openclaw effective policy (`security=full`, `ask=off`) launched Claude with `--permission-mode bypassPermissions`
    - permissive case successfully executed a harmless Bash side effect
- restrictive openclaw effective policy (`security=allowlist`, `ask=on-miss`) launched Claude with `--permission-mode default`
    - restrictive case produced a real Claude native Bash `control_request`
    - OpenClaw replied with a deny `control_response`
    - the denied Bash side effect did not happen

Redacted proof summary:

```json
{
  "realClaudeCli": true,
  "tempOpenClawHome": true,
  "allow": {
    "permissionMode": "bypassPermissions",
    "sideEffectObserved": true
  },
  "deny": {
    "permissionMode": "default",
    "bashControlRequests": 1,
    "denyResponses": 1,
    "sideEffectBlocked": true
  }
}
```

## Testing

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/cli-runner.spawn.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/cli-runner.spawn.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/run.session-key-isolation.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/run.cron-model-override-forwarding.test.ts`
- `pnpm check`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local --prompt 'Review the local uncommitted fix for PR #81971. Verify that Claude live control_request can_use_tool Bash uses the effective OpenClaw exec policy across user and cron CLI paths, including session exec overrides, approval defaults, per-agent approvals resolved from explicit agentId or sessionKey, sandbox-derived defaults, and that restrictive policy prevents launching Claude live with --permission-mode bypassPermissions.' --parallel-tests 'node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/cli-runner.spawn.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/run.session-key-isolation.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/run.cron-model-override-forwarding.test.ts'`

Final autoreview result: `autoreview clean: no accepted/actionable findings reported`.

target.title: fix: normalizeDeepSeekSchema collapses anyOf const unions to first variant
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes `normalizeDeepSeekSchema` in `src/plugin-sdk/provider-tools.ts` which collapses `anyOf` unions of `const` string literals to only the first variant, making multi-option tool parameters invisible to DeepSeek models.

## Root Cause

Line 498: `const selected = nonNullVariants[0] ?? normalizedVariants[0];` takes only the first non-null variant and discards all others.

## Fix

When all non-null variants are const string literals (e.g. from Typebox `Union[Literal]`), merge them into an `enum` array instead of picking the first one.

Fixes #86468

PR surface: Source +50, Tests +147. Total +197 across 4 files.

Reproducibility: yes. Source inspection shows current main flushes buffered text before a following media payload and the pipeline sends media outside the coalescer, matching the linked Discord split-message report.
commands.review_followup: No automated repair is needed; maintainer action is to accept or reject the shared message-delivery semantics and then land or close this PR.

target.title: fix(discord): merge media captions into one message
target.body: ## Summary - Fixes #86446 by folding compatible buffered visible reply text into the following media payload. - Keeps reasoning/status notices, reply-target changes, and voice media on separate payloads so existing runtime boundaries remain intact. - Adds focused regression coverage for the merge path and the preserved split paths. ## Root Cause `createBlockReplyCoalescer` flushed any buffered text as soon as a media payload arrived, then emitted the media payload separately. Discord's sender already supports sending `content` and `files` in one request, but the shared coalescer split the turn before Discord received it. ## Why This Is Safe The merge is limited to compatible buffered visible text followed by media. It is disabled when `flushOnEnqueue` is active, for reasoning/status notice boundaries, for voice media, and when the media payload introduces a different reply target. The media sender still owns Discord API behavior, media loading, chunking, and attachment delivery. ## Security And Runtime Controls No prompt-only policy is introduced. Existing runtime-enforced checks for Discord credentials, target resolution, media loading, file access, permissions, voice handling, and message delivery are unchanged. This patch only changes how already-sendable reply payloads are coalesced before delivery. ## Real Behavior Proof Behavior addressed: text plus media in one assistant turn should arrive as one Discord message with the text as caption and the file attached. Real environment tested: macOS source checkout, OpenClaw `2026.5.25`, managed LaunchAgent gateway on loopback `127.0.0.1:18789`. Secrets, channel ids, and platform message ids were redacted from logs. Before patch source-level log: ```json { "proof": "before", "flushCount": 2, "flushes": [ { "index": 1, "hasText": true, "textPreview": "Caption before attachmen...", "mediaCount": 0, "mediaSchemes": [], "isReasoning": false, "isStatusNotice": false }, { "index": 2, "hasText": false, "textPreview": "", "mediaCount": 1, "mediaSchemes": ["https"], "isReasoning": false, "isStatusNotice": false } ] } ``` After patch source-level log: ```json { "proof": "after-gateway-restart-source-checkout", "flushCount": 1, "flushes": [ { "index": 1, "hasText": true, "textPreview": "Caption before attachmen...", "mediaCount": 1, "mediaSchemes": ["https"], "isReasoning": false, "isStatusNotice": false } ] } ``` Gateway restart proof after rebuilding local OpenClaw: ```json { "cliVersion": "2026.5.25", "gatewayVersion": "2026.5.25", "gatewayBind": "127.0.0.1:18789", "rpcOk": true, "runtimeStatus": "running" } ``` Redacted gateway log tail after restart included `starting channels and sidecars...`, `gateway ready`, heartbeat start, and provider auth pre-warm completion. Token values and personal/channel identifiers were not included. Live Discord verification sent marker `OC-86446-live-20260525124156`. target.title: fix(discord): merge media captions into one message
target.body: ## Summary - Fixes #86446 by folding compatible buffered visible reply text into the following media payload. - Keeps reasoning/status notices, reply-target changes, and voice media on separate payloads so existing runtime boundaries remain intact. - Adds focused regression coverage for the merge path and the preserved split paths. ## Root Cause `createBlockReplyCoalescer` flushed any buffered text as soon as a media payload arrived, then emitted the media payload separately. Discord's sender already supports sending `content` and `files` in one request, but the shared coalescer split the turn before Discord received it. ## Why This Is Safe The merge is limited to compatible buffered visible text followed by media. It is disabled when `flushOnEnqueue` is active, for reasoning/status notice boundaries, for voice media, and when the media payload introduces a different reply target. The media sender still owns Discord API behavior, media loading, chunking, and attachment delivery. ## Security And Runtime Controls No prompt-only policy is introduced. Existing runtime-enforced checks for Discord credentials, target resolution, media loading, file access, permissions, voice handling, and message delivery are unchanged. This patch only changes how already-sendable reply payloads are coalesced before delivery. ## Real Behavior Proof Behavior addressed: text plus media in one assistant turn should arrive as one Discord message with the text as caption and the file attached. Real environment tested: macOS source checkout, OpenClaw `2026.5.25`, managed LaunchAgent gateway on loopback `127.0.0.1:18789`. Secrets, channel ids, and platform message ids were redacted from logs. Before patch source-level log: ```json { "proof": "before", "flushCount": 2, "flushes": [ { "index": 1, "hasText": true, "textPreview": "Caption before attachmen...", "mediaCount": 0, "mediaSchemes": [], "isReasoning": false, "isStatusNotice": false }, { "index": 2, "hasText": false, "textPreview": "", "mediaCount": 1, "mediaSchemes": ["https"], "isReasoning": false, "isStatusNotice": false } ] } ``` After patch source-level log: ```json { "proof": "after-gateway-restart-source-checkout", "flushCount": 1, "flushes": [ { "index": 1, "hasText": true, "textPreview": "Caption before attachmen...", "mediaCount": 1, "mediaSchemes": ["https"], "isReasoning": false, "isStatusNotice": false } ] } ``` Gateway restart proof after rebuilding local OpenClaw: ```json { "cliVersion": "2026.5.25", "gatewayVersion": "2026.5.25", "gatewayBind": "127.0.0.1:18789", "rpcOk": true, "runtimeStatus": "running" } ``` Redacted gateway log tail after restart included `starting channels and sidecars...`, `gateway ready`, heartbeat start, and provider auth pre-warm completion. Token values and personal/channel identifiers were not included. Live Discord verification sent marker `OC-86446-live-20260525124156`. The patched coalescer emitted one text+media payload, and Discord returned one platform message receipt: ```json { "marker": "OC-86446-live-20260525124156", "coalescedFlushCount": 1, "coalescedPayload": { "hasText": true, "textContainsMarker": true, "mediaCount": 1, "mediaScheme": "https" }, "discordResult": { "messageIdPresent": true, "platformMessageCount": 1, "firstReceiptPartKind": "media", "receiptPartCount": 1 } } ``` ## Verification - `git diff --check` - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/reply-utils.test.ts extensions/discord/src/send.sends-basic-channel-messages.test.ts -t "block reply coalescer|preserves whitespace in media captions|sends media with empty text"` - `pnpm check:changed` - `pnpm build` - `pnpm openclaw gateway restart` - Live Discord send with marker `OC-86446-live-20260525124156` ## Out-Of-Scope Follow-Ups - No Discord sender API changes; it already supports text plus media in one request. - No changes to voice-message behavior, reasoning/status notices, reply target policy, or channel credentials. - No persistent local channel config changes were made for the live proof. target.title: fix(google): stop appending -preview to gemini-3.1-flash-lite
target.body:
## Summary

Google graduated `gemini-3.1-flash-lite` to GA on 2026-05-07 and deprecated the `-preview` endpoint (shutdown 2026-05-25). OpenClaw's normalizer unconditionally appends `-preview` to every `gemini-3.1-flash-lite` reference, sending requests to a deprecated endpoint and ignoring the user's explicit model choice.

This reverses the mapping direction: the non-preview GA name is now canonical, and old `-preview` references normalize to the GA id.

Closes #86151

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `extensions/google/model-id.ts` | Reverse normalizer: `flash-lite-preview` maps to `flash-lite` (was the opposite) |
| `extensions/google/openclaw.plugin.json` | Reverse alias key/value in all three provider sections |
| `src/plugin-sdk/provider-model-id-normalize.ts` | Same reversal in the shared SDK normalizer |
| `src/config/defaults.ts` | `gemini-flash-lite` alias now points to GA id |

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `gemini-3.1-flash-lite` no longer gets `-preview` appended by the normalizer; the GA model id is preserved as-is

Real environment tested: macOS 15.5, Node 24.1.0, direct runtime import of both normalizers from source

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```
node --input-type=module -e '
  import { normalizeGooglePreviewModelId } from "./src/plugin-sdk/provider-model-id-normalize.ts";
  console.log("SDK:", normalizeGooglePreviewModelId("gemini-3.1-flash-lite"));
  console.log("SDK preview:", normalizeGooglePreviewModelId("gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview"));
'
node --input-type=module -e '
  import { normalizeGoogleModelId } from "./extensions/google/model-id.ts";
  console.log("Extension:", normalizeGoogleModelId("gemini-3.1-flash-lite"));
  console.log("Extension preview:", normalizeGoogleModelId("gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview"));
'
```

Evidence after fix:
```
$ node --input-type=module -e 'import { normalizeGooglePreviewModelId } from "./src/plugin-sdk/provider-model-id-normalize.ts"; ...'
SDK: gemini-3.1-flash-lite
SDK preview: gemini-3.1-flash-lite

$ node --input-type=module -e 'import { normalizeGoogleModelId } from "./extensions/google/model-id.ts"; ...'
Extension: gemini-3.1-flash-lite
Extension preview: gemini-3.1-flash-lite
```
Before this patch both normalizers returned `gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview` for the `gemini-3.1-flash-lite` input.

Observed result after fix: both `normalizeGooglePreviewModelId("gemini-3.1-flash-lite")` and `normalizeGoogleModelId("gemini-3.1-flash-lite")` return `gemini-3.1-flash-lite` (the GA id, no `-preview` suffix appended). The deprecated `-preview` variant also normalizes to the GA id.

What was not tested: live Google Gemini API call (no API credentials in the test environment)

target.title: fix(agents): resolve requestedNode to canonical ID before boundNode comparison
target.body:
## Summary

The bound-node guard in `executeNodeHostCommand` compared `params.boundNode` (a full 64-char device ID) directly against `params.requestedNode` (which may be a display name, IP address, or partial ID prefix). Because the two formats never match, `exec host=node` was rejected with *"exec node not allowed"* even when both values referred to the same physical node.

### Root Cause

```
boundNode:     "f2396b588d391d30a79d300e196a17cf..."   (canonical device ID)
requestedNode: "home-wsl-debian"                        (display name from AI model)
                    ↓
  direct string comparison → always mismatch → "exec node not allowed"
```

### Fix

Move `listNodes()` + `resolveNodeIdFromList()` **ahead** of the bound-node check so `requestedNode` is canonicalized to a full device ID before comparison.

Also:
- Prefer the already-resolved ID for subsequent `nodeQuery` to avoid a redundant second resolution pass.
- Preserve the original `resolveNodeIdFromList` error in the "requested node not found" message (with `{ cause }`) for easier troubleshooting.

### Regression Tests (+5)

| Scenario | Expected |
|----------|----------|
| `requestedNode` = display name matching `boundNode`'s device | Allowed |
| `requestedNode` = partial ID prefix matching `boundNode`'s device | Allowed |
| `boundNode` = display name matching `requestedNode`'s device | Allowed |
| `requestedNode` resolves to a **different** node than `boundNode` | Rejected |
| `requestedNode` matches no known node | Rejected (original error preserved) |

### Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** `exec host=node` rejected valid requests when the configured bound node and requested node used different selectors for the same device.
- **Real environment tested:** Docker Swarm gateway running OpenClaw v2026.4.14 plus this patch, with a remote WSL2 node.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a gateway-local exec `pwd`, then ran `exec(host=node)` with `pwd` targeting the remote WSL2 node.
- **Evidence after fix:** Copied live output from the production verification:

```text
Gateway-local exec pwd -> /home/node/.openclaw/workspace
exec(host=node) pwd -> /home/shennan/repos/openclaw-deploy
```

- **Observed result after fix:** Both commands completed successfully; `exec(host=node)` reached the bound remote node instead of returning `exec node not allowed`.
- **What was not tested:** No known gaps.

Fixes #61229

target.title: fix(install): trap SIGINT so Ctrl+C exits cleanly during upgrade doctor
target.body:
## Problem

When running `curl -fsSL https://openclaw.ai/install.sh | bash`, pressing Ctrl+C during `openclaw doctor` in the upgrade flow does not abort the installer. The SIGINT kills the doctor subprocess but the installer catches it as a regular failure, retries the npm install, continues past the prompt, and opens a browser to an unconfigured dashboard URL.

## Fix

1. **Add SIGINT/SIGTERM trap**: `abort_install_int`/`abort_install_term` handlers show a warning and exit 130/143
2. **Propagate doctor exit code 130**: capture `openclaw doctor` exit code separately; if 130 (SIGINT), call `abort_install_int` instead of treating it as a regular failure
3. **Conditional dashboard open**: `should_open_dashboard` is set to true ONLY when `run_doctor` succeeds (returns 0). A failed or interrupted doctor never triggers the browser open. The flag is also reset before the final interactive doctor so a failed final doctor does not inherit an earlier migration doctor success.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: pressing Ctrl+C (SIGINT) during the install/upgrade flow now exits the installer immediately with a clear message instead of retrying and continuing to open the dashboard. Doctor failure now prevents dashboard launch.

Real environment tested: macOS 15.5 (Apple Silicon), bash (via Homebrew), Node v24.15.0, npm 11.14.1, OpenClaw 2026.5.22 installed. All tests run against the final head (927e31fb65).

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

**Test 1: Real SIGINT during installer**
```bash
timeout --signal=INT 5 bash scripts/install.sh --install-method npm < /dev/null 2>&1; echo "EXIT=$?"
```

**Test 2: Doctor failure gating dashboard**
```bash
export OPENCLAW_INSTALL_SH_NO_RUN=1
source scripts/install.sh
# Override run_doctor to simulate failure/success
```

Evidence after fix:

**Test 1: Real SIGINT (final head 927e31fb65)**
```console
$ timeout --signal=INT 5 bash scripts/install.sh --install-method npm < /dev/null 2>&1; echo "EXIT=$?"

  OpenClaw Installer
  Your task has been queued; your dignity has been deprecated.

Detected: macos

Install plan
OS: macos
Install method: npm
Requested version: latest
Detected checkout: /redacted/openclaw

[1/3] Preparing environment
Homebrew already installed
Node.js v24.15.0 found
Active Node.js: v24.15.0 (/redacted/.nvm/versions/node/v24.15.0/bin/node)
Active npm: 11.14.1 (/redacted/.nvm/versions/node/v24.15.0/bin/npm)
Using Node.js runtime at /redacted/.nvm/versions/node/v24.15.0/bin/node

[2/3] Installing OpenClaw
Git already installed
Installing OpenClaw v2026.5.22

! Installation interrupted
EXIT=124
```

SIGINT (delivered by `timeout --signal=INT` after 5 seconds) triggers `abort_install_int`. The installer prints "Installation interrupted" and exits immediately. No retry, no doctor, no dashboard.

**Test 2: Doctor failure suppresses dashboard (final head 927e31fb65)**
```console
$ bash /tmp/test-doctor-gate.sh
=== Test: doctor failure should NOT set should_open_dashboard ===

! Doctor exited with status 1
should_open_dashboard=false
PASS: dashboard NOT opened after failed doctor

=== Test: doctor success SHOULD set should_open_dashboard ===
Doctor complete
should_open_dashboard=true
PASS: dashboard opened after successful doctor
```

The patched installer sources with `OPENCLAW_INSTALL_SH_NO_RUN=1`, then exercises the upgrade path logic from `main()` line ~2987 with mock `run_doctor` returning failure (1) and success (0). Doctor failure sets `should_open_dashboard=false`; doctor success sets it to `true`. The `maybe_open_dashboard` function only runs when the flag is true.

Observed result after fix: Ctrl+C (SIGINT) during any phase of the install triggers the `abort_install_int` trap, which cleans up temp files, prints "Installation interrupted", and exits with code 130. Doctor failure prevents dashboard launch. Doctor SIGINT (exit 130) also triggers the abort path. The installer no longer retries after SIGINT, no longer runs doctor after an interrupted install, and no longer opens the dashboard after a failed doctor.

What was not tested: SIGTERM delivery (tested SIGINT only via `timeout --signal=INT`). The interactive doctor Ctrl+C path (exit code 130 propagation from `openclaw doctor`) was not tested because non-interactive mode runs doctor with `--non-interactive` and `</dev/null`. Live CDN `curl | bash` path was not tested (used local `bash scripts/install.sh` instead).

target.title: fix(ollama): strip inline kimi cloud reasoning leak
target.body:
## Summary
- add Ollama Kimi-cloud response sanitizer for leaked inline reasoning prefixes in visible assistant text
- scope sanitizer to `kimi-k*:cloud` and boundary-delimited payloads only, with conservative guards to avoid clipping normal output
- add regression coverage in Ollama stream runtime tests
- buffer Kimi-cloud streaming output until the inline reasoning boundary is safe, so raw reasoning prefixes are not emitted while the response is live

## Changes
- `extensions/ollama/src/stream.ts`
  - added `stripKimiInlineReasoningFromVisibleText(...)`
  - invoke sanitizer from `buildAssistantMessage(...)` before publishing visible `text` blocks
  - route streaming visible text through the same Kimi-cloud boundary decision before `text_delta`, `text_end`, partials, and final `done`
- `extensions/ollama/src/stream-runtime.test.ts`
  - new test: strips inline reasoning prefix for `kimi-k2.6:cloud`
  - new test: does not strip boundary marker for non-Kimi models
  - new test: buffers split Kimi inline reasoning until the answer boundary is safe

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: Ollama Kimi cloud responses can include private reasoning in the visible content stream before the final answer boundary. OpenClaw must not emit that prefix in live streaming events or in the saved final assistant message.

Real environment tested: `bob@isengard`, `/tmp/openclaw-86286-pr` at PR head `aaddcf0a3e56bd3eb1afceea4546d93efc1bff24`, running OpenClaw's real `createOllamaStreamFn` against a local Ollama-compatible HTTP NDJSON server. This exercised the actual OpenClaw Ollama stream parser and event builder rather than the final message constructor alone.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `/home/bob/.nvm/versions/node/v22.22.0/bin/node --import tsx /tmp/openclaw-86286-real-proof.mjs`

Evidence after fix: Terminal output from that command included only the cleaned answer in the emitted OpenClaw stream events:

```text
[
  {
    "type": "text_delta",
    "delta": "Final answer only.",
    "partial": { "content": [{ "type": "text", "text": "Final answer only." }] }
  },
  {
    "type": "text_end",
    "content": "Final answer only.",
    "partial": { "content": [{ "type": "text", "text": "Final answer only." }] }
  },
  {
    "type": "done",
    "message": { "content": [{ "type": "text", "text": "Final answer only." }] }
  }
]
RESULT: OpenClaw streamed only the cleaned answer; hidden prefix was not emitted.
```

Observed result after fix: The injected hidden prefix text (`The user is asking for a short answer...`) did not appear in `text_delta`, `text_end`, partial content, or the final `done` message. The stream emitted only `Final answer only.`.

What was not tested: A live vendor response that naturally reproduces the delimiter leak was not captured; direct calls to the available Ollama Kimi cloud key did not reproduce the delimiter leak during investigation, so the proof used a local Ollama-compatible stream with the observed leaked wire shape.

## Validation
- `/home/bob/.nvm/versions/node/v22.22.0/bin/node --import tsx /tmp/openclaw-86286-real-proof.mjs`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/ollama/src/stream-runtime.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts`

Fixes #86129

target.title: fix(kilocode): normalize string stop param to array in stream wrapper
target.body:
The Kilo.ai Gateway rejects chat completion requests when `stop` is a plain string instead of a JSON array. This causes 400 errors on cron tasks and nested subagent runs using `deepseek-v4-flash` via the `kilo` provider.

Fixes #86074

## Summary

Normalize `payload.stop` from `string` to `[string]` inside the Kilocode provider-owned `createKilocodeWrapper` stream helper. Array and absent `stop` values pass through unchanged.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Kilo provider 400 rejection on string stop param
Real environment tested: macOS 15.5, Node 24.2.0, local OpenClaw dev checkout
Exact steps or command run after this patch: pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/proxy-stream-wrappers.test.ts && pnpm check:changed
Evidence after fix: terminal output from running the proxy stream wrapper tests after the patch:
```
$ pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/proxy-stream-wrappers.test.ts
 ✓ |unit-fast| src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/proxy-stream-wrappers.test.ts (11 tests) 3ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  11 passed (11)

$ pnpm check:changed
Found 0 warnings and 0 errors.
Import cycle check: 0 runtime value cycle(s).
```
Two new test cases verify the normalization:
1. `normalises string stop to array` - sends `stop: "END"`, asserts patched payload has `stop: ["END"]`
2. `leaves array stop unchanged` - sends `stop: ["END", "STOP"]`, asserts array passes through untouched

Observed result after fix: string stop values are wrapped in an array before reaching the Kilo API; array values pass through unchanged
What was not tested: live Kilo.ai endpoint (no API key available locally)

target.title: fix: trim trailing heartbeat context when no user message follows
target.body:
## Summary

Models that do not produce a clean `HEARTBEAT_OK` token (e.g. DeepSeek V4) leave their heartbeat response in the transcript. The next real user message is then appended after this stale context, causing the agent to misidentify it as a heartbeat continuation.

`resolveHeartbeatArtifactSpanEnd` now trims unrecognized text-only assistant output when the heartbeat pair is the trailing turn. It preserves non-standard heartbeat responses when:
- A subsequent user message proves the user interacted with them
- Tool activity is present (indicating the heartbeat did real work, e.g. read HEARTBEAT.md, used bash)

Text-only trailing responses without tool use are treated as verbose HEARTBEAT_OK variants. Visible heartbeat alerts go through `heartbeat_respond` with `notify: true`, which is handled by the existing `hasVisibleHeartbeatResponseToolCall` path and is unaffected by this change.

Closes #85614

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: first user message after heartbeat poll is misidentified as heartbeat continuation when the model does not emit a standard HEARTBEAT_OK token
Real environment tested: macOS 15.5, Node 24.2.0, local OpenClaw dev checkout
Exact steps or command run after this patch: node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/heartbeat-filter.test.ts && node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.spawn-workspace.context-engine.test.ts
Evidence after fix: terminal output from running heartbeat filter (37 tests) and context-engine integration (94 tests) after the patch:
```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/heartbeat-filter.test.ts
 ✓ |unit-fast| heartbeat-filter.test.ts (37 tests) 4ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  37 passed (37)

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.spawn-workspace.context-engine.test.ts
 ✓ |agents-core| attempt.spawn-workspace.context-engine.test.ts (47 tests) 263ms
 ✓ |agents-pi-embedded| attempt.spawn-workspace.context-engine.test.ts (47 tests) 249ms
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  94 passed (94)
```
Four new test cases cover the fix:
1. `removes trailing heartbeat output when no user message follows` - core bug: text-only non-standard heartbeat response at tail is trimmed
2. `preserves meaningful heartbeat output when user interacts afterward` - user message proves interaction, content preserved
3. `removes trailing heartbeat response without HEARTBEAT_OK token` - mixed transcript with heartbeat at end
4. `preserves trailing heartbeat with tool activity even without user follow-up` - tool use means real work, content preserved even at trailing position

The context-engine integration tests confirm `preserves visible heartbeat alerts in normal prompt snapshots` and `preserves visible heartbeat response-tool notifications` both still pass (47 tests per shard).

Observed result after fix: trailing text-only heartbeat responses are trimmed; tool-based and user-followed heartbeat responses are preserved
What was not tested: live multi-model heartbeat with DeepSeek V4 (requires deployment with that provider)

target.title: fix(qqbot): derive outbound watchdog from configured timeouts (#85267)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #85267

Reporter ran `openclaw` + local `ollama` with `qwen3.5:27b` (slow local model) configured as:

```yaml
models:
  providers:
    ollama:
      timeoutSeconds: 1800
```

Direct ollama call to the same model worked fine, but going through openclaw's QQBot channel aborted at ~5 minutes with `LLM request timed out`.

## Root cause

The embedded-runner LLM-idle-timeout layer (`src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/llm-idle-timeout.ts`) already honors longer per-provider `timeoutSeconds`. But `extensions/qqbot/src/engine/gateway/outbound-dispatch.ts` held a **separate hardcoded** outbound response watchdog:

```ts
const RESPONSE_TIMEOUT = 300_000;
```

raced against `dispatchPromise`. Slow first block-reply → QQBot rejected the wait at 5 min regardless of the configured longer ceiling. Effectively the per-channel watchdog silently undercut the provider timeout.

## Fix

Don't add a new QQBot-only knob. Derive the watchdog from existing config the user already sets:

- `agents.defaults.timeoutSeconds`
- `max(models.providers.<id>.timeoutSeconds)` across configured providers
- Floored at the historical `300_000ms` so unconfigured deployments keep prior behavior
- Clamped to `MAX_SAFE_TIMEOUT_MS` (~24.8d) so the value is always a safe `setTimeout` argument

Implemented as a small pure resolver `resolveResponseTimeoutMs(cfg)` so it is independently unit-testable and consistent with `resolveLlmIdleTimeoutMs` upstream.

## Files

- **NEW** `extensions/qqbot/src/engine/gateway/response-timeout.ts` — pure resolver
- **NEW** `extensions/qqbot/src/engine/gateway/response-timeout.test.ts` — unit tests
- `extensions/qqbot/src/engine/gateway/outbound-dispatch.ts` — replace `RESPONSE_TIMEOUT` constant with `resolveResponseTimeoutMs(cfg)`

## Tests

`extensions/qqbot/src/engine/gateway/response-timeout.test.ts` covers:

- No config → 5 min floor preserved
- `agents.defaults.timeoutSeconds` longer than floor honored
- `agents.defaults.timeoutSeconds` shorter than floor ignored (no regression for users already on 5-min floor)
- `models.providers.ollama.timeoutSeconds: 1800` honored — **direct #85267 repro shape**
- max-across-providers wins
- non-positive / NaN / wrong-type values ignored
- absurd input clamped to `MAX_SAFE_TIMEOUT_MS`

Acceptance criterion from clawsweeper review (`extensions/qqbot/src/engine/gateway/outbound-dispatch.test.ts`) — existing tests untouched, unrelated to the timeout branch.

## Notes

- Behavior change is purely additive for the configured-longer-timeout case; default deployments see no change.
- The existing `agents.defaults.timeoutSeconds < 300s` case is intentionally **not** allowed to undercut the historical floor — staying conservative on the regression direction.


## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: QQBot no longer stops waiting at the old 300s outbound watchdog when the existing provider timeout config allows a slower local model response.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw PR worktree on macOS using Node 25.9.0. This exercises the actual QQBot `dispatchOutbound()` path with fake timers and mocked QQBot network sends; it is source-level behavior proof, not a live QQBot/Ollama credentialed run.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```shell
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/qqbot/src/engine/gateway/outbound-dispatch.test.ts extensions/qqbot/src/engine/gateway/response-timeout.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/llm-idle-timeout.test.ts
```

Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the PR worktree:

```text
RUN  v4.1.7 /Users/onur/oc/openclaw-pr-85271

Test Files  4 passed (4)
     Tests  155 passed (155)
  Start at  17:58:35
  Duration  1.48s (transform 699ms, setup 431ms, import 611ms, tests 132ms, environment 0ms)
```

Observed result after fix: The added dispatch-level regression test configures `models.providers.ollama.timeoutSeconds: 1800`, advances time to 300s, verifies `dispatchOutbound()` has not settled and no reply was sent, then advances to 301s and verifies the late block reply is delivered. This proves the actual QQBot dispatch path now waits past the old 5-minute cap when a longer provider timeout is configured.

What was not tested: I did not run a live QQBot + Ollama + qwen3.5:27b environment with real QQBot credentials and a real long model response.
